title: Collage as Archive: Material Memory in the Cut
author: Ana Mendieta
summary: An essay on the archival impulse in collage practice, tracing the relationship between material accumulation and memory.
fulltext: The collage is an archive of fragments. Each torn edge, each overlapping surface, is a notation of encounter. When Schwitters assembled his Merzbau, he was not simply making art — he was constructing a memory palace from the detritus of modernity. The material logic of collage insists on preservation through transformation: nothing is lost, only relocated, re-contextualised, made strange by proximity to what was never supposed to touch it. This essay traces that archival impulse through the twentieth century.

title: Notation as Preparation: On the Pre-Novelistic Imagination
author: Georges Perec
summary: A meditation on the notebook, the index card, and the list as pre-novelistic forms — structures that precede the novel but already contain it.
fulltext: Before the novel, there is the notation. The writer fills notebooks not merely to remember but to think in a different register — one that is lateral rather than linear, associative rather than argumentative. Perec understood this: his infraordinaire was first a practice of notation, then a system of constraints, then finally the narrative scaffolding of Life: A User's Manual. The preparation is the work.

title: The Pleasure of the Text and the Archive of Reading
author: Roland Barthes
summary: Notes toward a phenomenology of reading as archival encounter — the reader as collector of textual remnants.
fulltext: Roland Barthes described the text as a tissue of quotations drawn from innumerable centres of culture. If the text is already an archive, then reading is a form of excavation. We do not consume texts; we add to them, annotate them, carry them into new contexts. The pleasure of the text is inseparable from the pleasure of accumulation — the bookmarked passage, the underlined sentence, the marginal notation that survives the original reading.

title: Epistemology of the Narrative Turn
author: Paul Ricoeur
summary: On the relationship between narrative and knowledge — how stories constitute ways of knowing.
fulltext: Narrative is not merely a form of representation — it is an epistemological structure. We do not narrate events that have already occurred in some narratively organised form; we organise events through the act of narration. Ricoeur's concept of emplotment (mise en intrigue) names this constitutive function: the narrative constructs the event as much as it records it. This has profound implications for the archive, for history, for any discipline that claims to tell what happened.

title: Invention and the Constraint: Oulipo and the Generative Limit
author: Raymond Queneau
summary: The paradox of constrained writing: how formal limitation becomes a generator of invention.
fulltext: The Oulipians understood that the blank page is not freedom but paralysis. The constraint — the lipogram, the N+7, the snowball — is not a restriction on invention but its precondition. By eliminating possibility, the constraint forces the writer into the territory they would never have explored voluntarily. Literary invention is not the absence of rules but their creative misapplication. Queneau's Exercises in Style demonstrates this most efficiently: one story, ninety-nine variations, infinite potential.
